skill.name: meta-baujat-plot
skill.description: Generate Baujat plots for heterogeneity analysis. Identify studies that contribute most to the overall meta-analysis results and heterogeneity, helping discover potential outlier studies. Input meta-analysis data CSV, output Baujat plot PNG and contribution data CSV.
skill.summary: Generate Baujat plots to detect outlier studies in meta-analysis by visualizing each study's contribution to overall results and heterogeneity.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 47 static findings: all are false positives when examined in context. The 18 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings match descriptive text and documentation, not cryptographic code. The 25 'shell backtick execution' findings match shell commands documented in SKILL.md markdown code blocks for legitimate R/Python script execution. The 2 'filesystem' findings are expected output-generation operations. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or credential theft patterns identified.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Documentation in Agent Instructions
low_findings.description: SKILL.md documents shell commands (Rscript, python, install.packages) inside markdown code blocks that the AI agent is instructed to execute. This is expected behavior for a data analysis skill that runs external scripts, but the agent has the ability to execute these commands with user-provided file paths.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write Operations for Output Generation
low_findings.description: The Python script creates output directories with os.makedirs and the R script uses dir.create. This is legitimate behavior for saving generated Baujat plot images and contribution data CSVs. No unauthorized file access or path traversal.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source Repository URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL to the GitHub source repository for attribution purposes. This is a documentation reference link and does not initiate any network requests during skill execution.

content.user_title: Generate Baujat Plots for Meta-Analysis Heterogeneity
content.value_statement: Researchers often struggle to identify which studies in a meta-analysis drive statistical heterogeneity. This skill generates Baujat plots that visually pinpoint outlier studies and quantify each study's contribution to overall heterogeneity and pooled results.
actual_capabilities: Reads meta-analysis data from CSV files with study-level outcomes
Generates Baujat plots as PNG images showing contribution to results vs heterogeneity
Supports binary, continuous, and survival data types
Outputs ranked contribution data as a CSV file sorted by heterogeneity contribution
Prints heterogeneity statistics including I-squared, Tau-squared, and Q statistic
Falls back to Python implementation when R environment is unavailable
limitations: Requires at least 3 studies in the input data for valid heterogeneity analysis
Python fallback only supports binary outcome data type
Does not perform sensitivity analysis or meta-regression
Cannot generate combined forest plots alongside the Baujat plot in a single output

use_cases.title: Identifying Outlier Studies in Published Research
use_cases.description: A medical researcher has collected 15 studies for a meta-analysis on treatment effectiveness. The skill generates a Baujat plot highlighting which studies contribute most to heterogeneity, helping the researcher decide whether to exclude outliers.

use_cases.title: Heterogeneity Assessment for Systematic Review
use_cases.description: A systematic review author needs to assess heterogeneity across 20 clinical trials. The skill provides both visual (Baujat plot) and quantitative (contribution CSV) output for the review manuscript.

use_cases.title: Teaching Meta-Analysis Diagnostic Methods
use_cases.description: A statistics educator demonstrates how individual studies affect overall heterogeneity using real datasets. The Baujat plot visually shows the relationship between study weight and heterogeneity contribution.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Baujat Plot Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Baujat plot from the meta-analysis data in [csv_path]. The data type is [Binary/Continuity/Survival].

prompt_templates.title: Baujat Plot with Specific Outcome Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Baujat plot using the file at [csv_path]. The data type is [Binary] and the outcome name is [outcome_name].

prompt_templates.title: Custom Output Directory Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a Baujat plot analysis on [csv_path] with data type [Survival]. Save the output to [output_dir] and name the outcome [outcome_name].
prompt_templates.scenario: Organizing output files in a specific folder

prompt_templates.title: Full Heterogeneity Assessment with Fallback
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a complete heterogeneity assessment on the data in [csv_path]. The data type is Binary. First try the R script, and if R is not available, use the Python fallback. Include the heterogeneity statistics in the output summary.

output_examples.input: User provides a CSV file with 10 studies on treatment X versus placebo, each with event counts and sample sizes.
output: Baujat plot PNG showing 10 study points with study labels, quadrant lines at median values, and color-coded outlier indicators
Contribution data CSV with columns for study name, contribution to result, contribution to heterogeneity, and rank
Text summary showing I-squared = 45.2%, Tau-squared = 0.1234, Q = 18.7 with p-value, and ranked list of studies

output_examples.input: User provides survival outcome data with hazard ratios and confidence intervals for 8 studies.
output: Baujat plot PNG for HR data with outlier studies highlighted in red
Ranked contribution CSV identifying the top 3 studies driving heterogeneity
Recommendation text flagging potential outlier studies for sensitivity analysis

best_practices: Validate input CSV columns match the expected data type schema before running the analysis to avoid script errors.
Review the ranked contribution CSV alongside the Baujat plot to identify studies that are both high-contribution and high-heterogeneity.
Run a sensitivity analysis by removing flagged outlier studies and re-running the Baujat plot to assess robustness.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for datasets with fewer than 3 studies, as heterogeneity statistics are not meaningful.
Do not rely solely on the Baujat plot to exclude studies, always combine with domain expertise and sensitivity analysis.
Do not mix different data types in a single CSV input, the analysis requires uniform study type data.

faq.question: What data format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The input must be a CSV file with study-level data. The required columns depend on the data type. Binary data needs study, group1_Events, group1_sample_size, group2_Events, group2_sample_size. Continuous data needs means and standard deviations. Survival data needs hazard ratios with confidence intervals.

faq.question: How many studies are needed for a valid analysis?
faq.answer: At least 3 studies are required. Fewer than 3 studies cannot produce meaningful heterogeneity statistics or a useful Baujat plot.

faq.question: What if R is not installed on my system?
faq.answer: A Python fallback script is included that handles binary outcome data. For continuous and survival data types, R with the meta and metafor packages is required.

faq.question: What output files are produced?
faq.answer: The skill produces two output files: a Baujat plot as a PNG image and a contribution data CSV file with study rankings.

faq.question: How do I interpret the Baujat plot?
faq.answer: The X-axis shows each study's contribution to the overall pooled result. The Y-axis shows contribution to heterogeneity. Studies in the upper right quadrant are potential outliers that both influence the result and increase heterogeneity.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for any type of medical research data?
faq.answer: This skill supports binary outcomes (odds ratios), continuous outcomes (standardized mean differences), and survival data (hazard ratios). It is designed for standard two-arm study comparisons in meta-analysis.
